_comment: Bugfix bake-off narrative deck — slidey spec scaffold. Scene types (title / cards / narrative) match stories/slidey-edit/baked/deck.json + stories/slidey-edit/schemas/deck.json so it bakes to static HTML via `slidey bundle` (see deck/README.md). Every number is a placeholder marked 'TBD from summary.json' until the live grid runs and the report fill step populates docs/case-studies/bugfix-bakeoff.md.

scenes.title: The Bugfix Bake-off
scenes.subtitle: Is structure worth more than a bigger model?
scenes.narration: We hold the bugs fixed and vary two things — the structure of the workflow, and the model behind it — then grade every run against a hidden oracle.

scenes.lede: One big prompt, plus a few nudges.
scenes.body: The default way to fix a bug with an agent is a single multi-stage prompt: reproduce, implement, test, all in one breath — and when it falls short, you nudge it. Up to five oracle-gated guidance turns. No enforced order, no typed artifacts, no boundary that fails closed.
scenes.narration: The naive baseline is one multi-stage prompt plus up to five guidance turns. Nothing enforces the order, and nothing fails closed when a stage is skipped.

scenes.lede: The same work, as a state machine.
scenes.body: Kitsoki's bugfix story is a seven-room pipeline: reproduce → propose → implement → test → review → validate → done. Every boundary is deterministic, every phase emits a typed artifact, and a failing test — not a verdict — is the reproduction. Same model, different structure.
scenes.narration: The kitsoki treatment drives the seven-room bugfix pipeline. Every phase boundary is deterministic and emits a typed artifact, and a failing test is the reproduction.

scenes.title: The experiment: a 2×4 grid
scenes.narration: For each of five real bugs we run a two-by-four grid: two treatments by four candidates, all from the fix's hermetic parent commit, graded against the fix's own regression test as a hidden oracle.

scenes.title: Results grid
scenes.narration: Each bug yields eight cells. Solve rates fill in here once the live grid runs.

scenes.body: The kitsoki treatment beats the single prompt by TBD from summary.json points of solve rate — at a fraction of the guidance turns, because each turn a single prompt burns is a failed oracle gate the pipeline catches at a room boundary.
scenes.narration: On the structure axis, the pipeline lifts the solve rate by a margin to be filled from the results, and it spends far fewer guidance turns getting there.

scenes.body: Across the four candidates the solve-rate spread is TBD from summary.json — measured here against the structure gap. If structure moves the needle more than swapping models does, structure is the lever and model is the cost knob.
scenes.narration: On the model axis, the spread between the strongest and weakest model is to be filled from the results — and compared head to head against what structure alone buys.

scenes.body: Cost divided by solve rate is the honest unit. A high solve rate bought with runaway tokens — the reprocessing tax of a long single-prompt loop — costs more per fix than a structured run that calls the model only where judgement is needed. Numbers: TBD from summary.json.
scenes.narration: The honest unit is cost per solved bug. A single prompt that wanders pays the reprocessing tax; the pipeline pays for judgement, not for replaying the conversation.

scenes.title: The cheaper-model-with-structure result
scenes.narration: The money question: can a cheaper model with the pipeline match a frontier model without it?

scenes.lede: Structure is the lever. Model is the cost knob.
scenes.body: The bake-off framework is reusable: drop new bugs into bakeoff.yaml, point it at new candidates, and regrade against the hermetic baselines and hidden oracles. Offline report regen runs with zero re-spend.
scenes.narration: If a cheaper model with structure matches a frontier model without it, structure is the lever and model choice is just the cost knob. The framework is reusable — add bugs, add candidates, regrade.
